content.text:
Here are the results for the search query about the paper by Samuel Marks and Max Tegmark:

## "The Geometry of Truth: Emergent Linear Structure in Large Language Model Representations of True/False Datasets"

**Authors:**
Samuel Marks (Northeastern University) and Max Tegmark (MIT)
**Paper:** Available on arXiv as [2310.06824](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06824),
first submitted on October 10, 2023, with the latest version (v3) updated on August 19, 2024

.

### Summary
Large Language Models (LLMs) have impressive capabilities, but are prone to outputting falsehoods.
Recent work has developed techniques for inferring whether a LLM is telling the truth by training probes on the LLM's internal activations.
However, this line of work is controversial, with some authors pointing out failures of these probes to generalize in basic ways, among other conceptual issues.

### Three Lines of Evidence

The paper draws on three types of evidence:

1.
Visualizations of LLM true/false statement representations, which reveal clear linear structure.
Transfer experiments in which probes trained on one dataset generalize to different datasets.
Causal evidence obtained by surgically intervening in a LLM's forward pass, causing it to treat false statements as true and vice versa.
### Key Findings
Overall, the authors present evidence that at sufficient scale, LLMs linearly represent the truth or falsehood of factual statements.
They also show that simple difference-in-mean probes generalize as well as other probing techniques while identifying directions which are more causally implicated in model outputs.

### Venue

The paper was submitted to OpenReview (initially for ICLR 2024 review).
The TL;DR describes it as: "Investigates how LLMs represent the difference between true and false statements, and improves techniques for extracting LLM beliefs."
An earlier version also
introduced a novel technique, mass-mean probing, which generalizes better and is more causally implicated in model outputs than other probing techniques.

Note: The query mentioned "COLM 2024" — this paper appears to have been published/presented at a venue in 2024, though the search results primarily show it on arXiv and OpenReview. The paper has been influential and is cited by subsequent work on truthfulness probing in LLMs.
